2 min read
Just now

--

The framework gives you everything search engines want. It also gives you enough rope to undo it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Is Next.js good for SEO? Yes — with one big caveat. The framework gives you everything search engines want, and just enough rope to undo all of it.
The question gets asked as if frameworks rank pages. They don’t. What a framework can do is remove or create technical obstacles, and on that score Next.js is unusually generous — it hands you a fast, crawlable, well-tagged site as the default path, then trusts you not to wander off it.
What does it give you for free
Server-rendered HTML means the content is in the page when a crawler — or an AI engine — arrives, not assembled later by JavaScript that a bot may never run. The Metadata API puts titles, descriptions, and canonicals in code, no plugin to misconfigure. Sitemaps and robots generate themselves. Images and fonts come with performance built in. Individually, these are conveniences; together, they’re most of technical SEO, handled.
The rope
The same flexibility that makes Next.js powerful makes it easy to sabotage. Reach for 'use client' on everything, and you’ve pushed your content back into JavaScript, hiding it from the crawlers you wanted to impress. Skip the image and font tools, and your Core Web Vitals — a real ranking signal — quietly collapse. The framework won’t stop you; it assumes you know what you’re doing.
The honest answer
Next.js is one of the best SEO frameworks in 2026 for teams who use it as intended: server-first, metadata in code, performance tools on. For a content-only site, Astro may ship even less JavaScript, and for a non-technical team, WordPress is still kinder. But if you want one framework that ranks a marketing site and runs an app, and you’re willing to stay on the server-rendered path, it’s hard to beat.
If you want that path made easy, the DesignToCodes Next.js templates ship SEO-ready.
